content.full_text:
Califcr:aia
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
May 8, 199 0
Stan■ey E. Stee■e
■3555 P■antation Way
Moreno Va■ley′  cA  92360
Re:  Your RequeSt for Advice
Our File No. A-90-254
Dear Mr. Steele:
You have requested advice concerning application of the
conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
trActr) 1 to your duties as a planning cornroissioner for the city of
Moreno Vai:ey.
OUESTION
You own a hone approxirnately 9oo feet from a proposed project
entitted the rrMoreno llighlands Specific PIan.rr May you
participate in discussions or decisions regardinq this proposed
proj ect?
CONCLUSION
under the Act, you maY not participate in your official
capacity in any discussion or decision regarding the uoreno
ttigtrlanas project if there will be a financial effect of $10,0o0
or more on the fair narket value of your property.
FACTS
You are a planning couutissioner for the city of lloreno vaL-
Iey. Your resj-dence, which you and your wj.fe own in fee, is
l-ocated approxinately 900 feet from the boundary of a proPosed
project called the truoreno Hightands specific Plan.I' The area of
the proposed project is currently unirnproved land on $rhich the
developer plans to build a 3,038-acre master-planned community
which will consist of approxirnately 554 acres of Planned business
center and 8,458 residential dwellings "linked together through a
1 cor"rrr."nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California code of Regulations Section
18OOO, et seo. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the california Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite EOO . P.O. Box 8O7 . Sacramenro CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916)322-5660


File No. A-9 0-2 54
Page 2
comprehensive network of parks, greenbelts, golf course and open
space. rr
since the Moreno Highlands Specific PIan differs from the
city's general pIan, a general plan amendment is also being
processed for the applicant.
ANALYSI S
The Act prohibits a public offici.el from making, par'-i.cipat-
j.ng j.n, or using hls of f icial position :o .j-nf luence a governmentai
decision in which he knotrs or has reason to know he has a
financiaf interest. 
(Section 87100.) A public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable frou its effect on the public generaLly, on the
official or a member of his imnediate fanily or on, among other
things:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investuent worth
one thousand dollars ($1000) or IDore.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1ooo) or nore.
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a conmercial lending
institution in the regular course of busj.ness on
terns available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating tlro hundred fifty doI-
lars (9250) or Dore in value provided to, received
by or pronised to the public official within 12
months prior to the tine when the decision is nade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
enployee, or holds any position of management.
section 87103 (a) -(d) .
Your residence is an interest in real property, as defined in
Section 82033 rrhich states:
rrlnterest in real propertyri includes any
leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an
option to acquire srtch an interest in real property
located in the jurrsdiction orrned directly,
indirectly or beneficially by the public official,
or other fiIer, or his or her irnmediate fanily if
the fair narket value of the interest is one
thousand dollars (S1,000) or utore....


FiIe No. A-90-254
Paqe 3
Foreseeabi l itv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it vi1I occur. certainty is not
required; horrever, an effect that is merely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Dorrnev Cares v. Do$mev Communitv
Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.. App. 3d 983; In re Thorner 1 FPPC
ops. 198. )
llaterialr-+-'l
The Cornmission has adopted several regulations which define
material financial effect. 
Regulation 18702 sets forth the
general guidelines for deternining whether an official's 
financial
interest in a decision is rrnaterialr as required by Section 87103.
If the official's 
financial interest is directlv involved i.n the
decision, Regulation L8702.1 applies to deternine nateriality. 
If
the official's 
financial interest is indirectlv affected by the
decision, Regutations fa7o2.2 through \8702.6 apply to determine
lrhether the effect of the decision is uaterial.
Regulation L8702.3 (copy enclosed) contains guidelines for
determining when the effect of a decision is material as to real
property vhich is indirectly involved in the decision. Regulation
18702.3 states in pertinent part:
(a) The effect of a decision is uaterial as
to real property in which an official 
has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the fo1-
Iowing appl ies:
(1) The real property in shich the of-
ficial 
has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within a 300 foot
radj.us of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official's 
real property interest.
(2) The decision involves construction
of, or iurprovenents to, streets, lrater, sei{er,
storn drainage or sinilar facilities, 
and the
real property in which the official 
has an
interest will receive new or substantially
improved servj,ces.
(3) The real property in which the of-
ficial has an interest is located outside a
radius of 30o feet and any part of the real
property is located rrithin a radius of 2,500
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the


File No. A-9o-254
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,OO0)
or lnore on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official 
has
an interesti or
(ts) Will affect the rental value of
che property by $1,000 or nore per 12
nonth period.
Your resj.dence is located approximately 900 feet from the
proposed project. If it is reasonably foreseeable that the ltoreno
Highlands project will have an effect of $10,000 or more on the
fair narket value of your hoDe, you may nbt participate in any
governmental decisions concerning this project.
You stated that you have not retained an independent real
estate appraiser to appraise your property and do not anticipate
that you wi1l, do so in the future. 
Under Regulation 18702.3(d),
there are a number of factors rrhich sha1l be considered in
deternining whether the decision will have a financial effect of
910,000 or more. These factors include, but are not lirnited to:
(1) The proxinity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the nagnitude of
the proposed project or change in use in relation-
ship to the property in which the official 
has an
interest;
(2) Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision vil1 affect the developnent potential
or income producing potential of the property;
(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood includ-
ing, but not linited to, effects on traffic, 
view,
privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emis-
sions, or sinilar traits of the neighborhood.
We are not in a position to confirn or reject your contention
regarding the financial effect of the proposed project on the fair
market value of your home. Therefore, it is incunbent upon you to
consider the above factors and to nake a good faith estinate of
the financial effect. 
If the proposed project will not have an
effect of $10,000 or Dore on the fair market value of your home,
you nay participate in the decisions concerning this project.
Holrever, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed project
will have a financial effect of 910,000 or Dore, you Eay not
participate in your official capacity.


File No. A-90-254
Page 5
Participatincr in a Non-official capacitv
Both Regrulation 18700(d) (2) and Regulation 1870o.1(b) (1)
(copies enclosed) allow a public official to appear, as any member
of the general public, before an agency in the course of its
prescrS.bed governmental function, to represent hj,nself or herself
on matters related solely to his or her personal interests. 
A
tipersonal interesttr includes an interest tn real property which is
wholly owned by the official or members of his or her inmediate
farnily. (Regulatj.on i8700.1(b) (i) (A) .)
Re$rtation 18700.1(b) (2) further aI1ows a disqualified public
official to cornmunicate with the general public or the press.
Thus, the Act allows you to cornment, as a rneuber of the public, at
public meetings, forums, hearings or to the press on issues
related solely to the effect of a decision on your property. once
you have fonnally disgualified yourself by announcing your
disgual ification and the reason therefor on the public record
(Regulation 18700(b) (5)), your comments should be nade under the
sane procedures applicable to comraents from nembers of the public.
For example, you are required to address the planning cornmission
from the audience, rather than from your conmissioner's seat.
Regrulations 18700 and 18700.1 peruit you to testify concerning the
effects of the proposed project on your property, but you may not
testify on behalf of any other person or group.
I trust thi.s answers your question. If you have any further
questions regarding this natter, please contact ne at (916)
322-590]-.
sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General counsel
By:盤製盤
ision
KED: JRS : p1h
Enclosures
